title: Chapter 8 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of Classes & OOP in TypeScript!

questions.question: What specifically is a Class in TypeScript/JavaScript?
options: A function that returns exactly an array.
A strict blueprint or template specifically designed to instantiate/create detailed objects equipped with predefined data and built-in methods.
A CSS style definition applied to HTML files.
A compiler flag for faster builds.
questions.explanation: Classes operate as object factories, standardizing exactly what properties and behaviors a new object will possess.

questions.question: A massive benefit of Classes is that the _____ runs automatically exactly once immediately when the `new` instance is generated to apply setup configurations.
options: destructor
generator
constructor
builder
questions.explanation: The constructor acts as the initial setup engine. (e.g. `constructor(title: string) { ... }`)

questions.question: What is the primary role of the `this` keyword inside a class?
options: It points directly to the core Node server environment.
It references the local function parameters instead of the class properties.
It strictly points to the single exact instance of the object actively executing the current code.
It points to the overarching class definition itself, ignoring instances.
questions.explanation: If Player #2 runs `this.score = 5`, it ensures Player #2's specific instance updates its score data, leaving Player #1 alone.

questions.question: If you fail to use `this.name` inside a method and simply type `name`, what frequently happens?
options: It correctly accesses the property.
It deletes the property forever.
It looks for a globally scope arbitrary variable called 'name' and horribly fails.
It dynamically generates an interface.
questions.explanation: Variables inside a class essentially only exist bound to the `this` context. Without `this.`, JS thinks you are looking for external variables.

questions.question: Which visibility modifier is applied by default natively logically if you write absolutely nothing before a property in a standard class?
options: private
protected
public
readonly
questions.explanation: Unless shielded explicitly, every variable in a JS class is utterly `public` and exposed globally.

questions.question: What is the crucial primary advantage of explicitly marking a property `private`?
options: It forces global external code to interact via sanctioned methods (like `setScore`), granting absolute control over exactly how the data is modified.
It compresses the data size, accelerating load times enormously.
It enables you to use the property across multiple unrelated different classes.
It encrypts the internal variable fully automatically with AES-256.
questions.explanation: Encapsulation (`private`) is massive for stability. It prevents reckless modifications (like a score magically becoming `-100`).

questions.question: A `protected` property remains utterly invisible to external foreign code precisely like `private`, but remains completely accessible conceptually to internal subclass logic extending the parent.
questions.explanation: Yes, `protected` strikes the balance: protecting from the outside, while permitting subclass inheritance access.

questions.question: What occurs inherently if global generic code attempts to call `account.balance` when `balance` is strictly marked `private`?
options: TypeScript deletes the class instantly.
It returns null instead.
TypeScript completely refuses to compile, citing visibility violations.
It automatically upgrades it to public.
questions.explanation: TS strictly enforces visibility modifiers at completely at compile time.

questions.question: How inherently does `readonly` fundamentally differ structurally from `private`?
options: `private` means nobody can see it; `readonly` means everybody can see it but nobody can edit it.
`readonly` means nobody can see it; `private` means everybody can edit it.
There is no difference structurally.
`readonly` is only applicable to boolean parameters dynamically.
questions.explanation: `readonly` allows public visibility, ensuring everyone can safely read an `id`, but strictly prohibits any reassignment modifications permanently.

questions.question: You can absolutely utilize the parameter shorthand properties notation (like `constructor(public name: string) {}`) to instantly define identical class attributes massively cleaning up syntax.
questions.explanation: This extensively used TS shortcut replaces manually writing the property, passing it to the constructor, and then writing `this.name = name;`.

questions.question: Which crucial keyword heavily dictates strongly that a given defined class fundamentally MUST mathematically satisfy all method obligations detailed heavily in a previously created Interface?
options: inherits
extends
implements
borrows
questions.explanation: You `implement` an Interface perfectly to strictly guarantee a rigorous comprehensive API contract.

questions.question: What invariably happens if a developer creates `class Car implements Vehicle`, but utterly fails completely to physically write the required `drive()` method mandated structurally by the `Vehicle` Interface?
options: TypeScript immediately refuses to rigorously compile, highlighting unequivocally that the explicit interface structural contract has been broken.
The class functions perfectly fine but logs a warning.
TypeScript magically creates a dummy function method seamlessly.
The application crashes the browser natively.
questions.explanation: Interfaces act as draconian blueprints. Breaking their stringent structural promises results directly in severe heavy compilation failures.

questions.question: Can a class mathematically implement exactly dual/multiple completely distinct Interfaces concurrently?
options: No, multiple implementation is fundamentally strictly banned natively.
Yes, by heavily wrapping them strictly in parentheses: `implements (A, B)`
Yes, by broadly separating them natively with commas: `implements A, B`
No, because TypeScript interfaces are conceptually mutually exclusive strictly.
questions.explanation: Unlike `extends`, you can rigidly `implement` countless heavily varied interfaces simultaneously to enforce dense architectural requirements.

questions.question: You heavily can explicitly assign an initialized instantiated class object instance into a variable completely generically using the class name strictly as its rigorous Type (e.g. `let myDog: Dog;`).
questions.explanation: Yes, Classes natively serve functionally dual roles as incredibly powerful specific Object Factories AND strictly rigid structured valid TypeScript Types equivalently.

questions.question: What is the primary crucial fundamental difference explicitly between defining a standard standalone generic Function versus defining a specific internal Class Method?
options: Methods must always return strings.

Methods literally exist explicitly physically housed inherently inside the class and inherently exclusively operate fundamentally conceptually upon the internal instance state specifically using `this`.
Functions inherently execute natively comprehensively faster extensively.
Methods rigorously cannot mathematically accept parameters natively.
questions.explanation: Methods deeply intertwine strictly comprehensively with the internal local data properties directly intrinsically possessed solely individually exclusively by the active object comprehensively natively.

questions.question: To firmly permanently structurally rigorously forbid all conceptual comprehensive modification arbitrarily fundamentally after construction conclusively intrinsically entirely, mark a specific class property rigorously strictly definitively as `____`.
options: const
locked
readonly
static
questions.explanation: The `readonly` modifier fundamentally intrinsically protects deeply unequivocally structurally property data exclusively perfectly.
